summaries.content: This lesson covers the poem 'A Watering Rhyme' from the Marigold textbook. The poem teaches children the correct way to take care of plants by watering them at the right time. It explains that early morning and evening are the best times to water plants. Watering during the hot afternoon sun is harmful because the water can heat up on the leaves and damage them. The poem uses simple rhyming words to make the message easy to remember, encouraging young learners to develop a habit of caring for plants and gardens.
keyPoints: The best times to water plants are early morning and evening
Watering during the hot afternoon sun can harm the plants
Water on leaves in strong sunlight can burn them
Plants need regular care and attention to grow healthy
The poem uses rhyming words to make the lesson fun and memorable

summaries.content: This lesson covers the story 'The Giving Tree' from the Marigold textbook. The story is about a tree that loved a little boy. When the boy was young, he would climb the tree, swing from its branches, eat its apples, and rest in its shade. As the boy grew older, he visited less often and wanted more. The tree gave him its apples to sell for money, its branches to build a house, and its trunk to make a boat. In the end, only a stump remained. When the boy returned as a tired old man, the stump offered itself as a seat. The tree was always happy to give. The story teaches selfless love, generosity, and the importance of being grateful to those who give us so much.
keyPoints: A tree and a boy had a loving friendship since the boy was little
The boy enjoyed playing under the tree during his childhood
As the boy grew, he needed money, a house, and a boat - the tree gave all it could
In the end, only the stump remained and the old man sat on it to rest
The tree represents selfless love, like that of parents or nature

summaries.content: This lesson focuses on the grammar topic of tenses covered in Unit 7. Tenses tell us when an action takes place. The three main tenses are: Past Tense (actions that already happened, e.g., 'The boy climbed the tree'), Present Tense (actions happening now or regularly, e.g., 'The tree gives shade'), and Future Tense (actions that will happen, e.g., 'I will water the plants tomorrow'). Students learn to form past tense by adding '-ed' to regular verbs and recognise irregular past tense forms. They also practise using 'will' or 'shall' to form the future tense.
keyPoints: Tenses tell us when an action happens: past, present, or future
Past tense: already happened - add '-ed' to regular verbs (walked, played)
Some verbs have irregular past forms (gave, sat, came, went)
Present tense: happening now or regularly (walks, plays, gives)
Future tense: will happen later - use 'will' or 'shall' before the verb

summaries.content: This lesson brings together the themes of Unit 7 - caring for nature and understanding tenses through the texts. Students explore how both the poem and the story highlight the relationship between humans and nature. The watering rhyme encourages us to care for plants, while the giving tree reminds us to be grateful for what nature provides. Grammar exercises use sentences from both texts to practise identifying and changing tenses, helping students connect language skills with meaningful stories.
keyPoints: Both the poem and story highlight our relationship with nature
The poem teaches us to care for plants; the story teaches gratitude
Tense exercises use examples from the poem and story
Changing verbs between past, present, and future tenses
Connecting grammar skills with real reading and comprehension
